sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 83, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Game still running after rotation. Character horizontal at [11,25]x[11,25] with feet on left. Green frame complete. Previous "game over" was temporary.
- Goal model: Unclear - need to find configuration that completes level without exceeding action limit.
- Plan: Try moving RIGHT from initial position to test boundary, then try different orientations.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, the character moved RIGHT 4 times. The character is now at [11, 25]x[29, 43]. The green frame is at [29, 49]x[41, 49]. The character's column range [29, 43] overlaps with the green frame's column range [41, 49] at columns [41, 43].
Looking at the image, the character is now overlapping with the green frame horizontally. The character's feet (red) are on the left side of the character.
Now the character is at rows [11, 25] and the green frame is at rows [29, 49]. The character is above the green frame. The character can't move DOWN from row 11, but maybe it can move DOWN from row 25 or other positions.
Wait, the character's row range is [11, 25]. The bottom of the character is at row 25. The green frame starts at row 29. So the character is 4 rows above the green frame.
Let me try moving DOWN from the current position to see if the character can move DOWN now.
Actually, looking at the image, the character is horizontal with feet on the left. The character's body is white and the feet are red. The character is at the top of the green frame.
Let me try moving DOWN to see if the character can overlap with the green frame.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Character horizontal at [11,25]x[29,43] with feet on left. Green frame at [29,49]x[41,49]. Character horizontally overlaps green frame at cols [41,43]. Character is 4 rows above green frame.
Plan: Try moving DOWN to overlap character with green frame vertically.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving DOWN from current position
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"Step: {current_frame.step}, Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}, Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"White: rows [{min(rows)}, {max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}, {max(cols)}]")
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Green: rows [{min(rows)}, {max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}, {max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Step: 83, Done: False, Game over: False
White: rows [14, 28], cols [29, 43]
Green: rows [29, 49], cols [41, 49], pixels=144
